cuisine: Western Restaurant — British-inspired (Singapore hawker and casual dining adaptation)
region_of_origin: United Kingdom (origin); Singapore (local adaptation using dory, basa, or barramundi)
food_subgroup: Battered deep-fried white fish fillet with thick-cut potato chips; mixed plate; high-calorie, high-fat, high-GL Western restaurant dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for fish and chips as a composite prepared dish. No exact FoodOn term covers the Singapore restaurant variant. Closest parent would be a battered fried fish food product, but this is not formally coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, battered and deep-fried (closest parent match; FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for fish and chips as a composite dish)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GZA covers battered deep-fried fish as the primary component. The potato chips component is not separately coded here; the code is used as the dominant food anchor for the dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish and chips as a prepared composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate comprising approximately 120–180g battered deep-fried white fish fillet, 150–200g thick-cut potato chips, and 30–80g tartar sauce, totalling approximately 450g as commonly served at Singapore casual dining restaurants and hawker Western stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Fish & Chips — Component Calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (battered fried fish, deep-fried potato) and HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation: battered deep-fried white fish fillet (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 174162, fish fillet battered and fried) combined with deep-fried thick-cut potato chips (USDA SR Legacy potato fries data) and tartar sauce (HPB Singapore). Weighted average per 100g of the composite dish calculated from a representative plate composition of 120g fish + 150g chips + 30g tartar sauce = 300g edible portion (excluding coleslaw for core calculation). Singapore restaurants typically use dory (Pangasius), basa, or barramundi rather than cod or haddock as used in the UK original.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Deep-Fried Battered Fish and Potato Chips — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (fish in batter GI ~38; French fries / potato chips GI ~75; weighted composite for Singapore fish and chips plate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore-style fish and chips as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~68 using a weighted composite approach: battered fried fish (GI ~38, University of Sydney GI Database, closest analogous entry for battered fried fish) combined with deep-fried thick-cut potato chips (GI ~75, University of Sydney GI Database, French fries / fried potato entry). Weighted by carbohydrate contribution: fish batter ~25g CHO per serving, chips ~47g CHO per serving, total ~72g CHO. Composite GI = (25×38 + 47×75) / 72 ≈ 62; adjusted upward to ~68 to account for full gelatinisation of batter starch and high surface-area frying of chips. GL per plate = GI × (72g CHO / 100) = 68 × 0.72 ≈ 49. This is classified as medium GI (55–69) but very high GL, representing one of the highest GL values in the Singapore Western restaurant category.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium load (~900mg per plate from batter seasoning, chip seasoning, and tartar sauce) combined with high caloric density and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to approximately 900mg sodium per plate (40% of the 2300mg daily limit); if consumed, request unseasoned chips and tartar sauce on the side to reduce sodium by up to 300–400mg, and prefer canola-oil-fried preparations over palm oil to minimise saturated fat co-exposure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~49 per plate) from combined batter starch and potato chip starch, representing one of the highest GL values among Singapore Western restaurant dishes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; the GL of ~49 per standard plate will cause a significant and sustained postprandial glucose excursion, and if the dish is consumed, substituting chips with a side salad or steamed vegetables reduces the GL from ~49 to approximately 8 (fish fillet alone), while choosing grilled fish without batter eliminates the batter starch contribution entirely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (14.1g/100g, ~63g per plate), elevated sodium (~900mg per plate), high caloric density (~760 kcal per plate), and lipid oxidation products generated during deep-frying at high temperatures
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish; the combination of high total fat, high sodium, very high caloric density, and deep-frying-derived lipid oxidation products (aldehydes from polyunsaturated frying oils) presents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk, and the dory/basa fish used in Singapore provides negligible EPA+DHA omega-3 fatty acids (~0.2g per serving), offering none of the cardioprotective benefit associated with oily fish such as salmon or mackerel.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GL (~49) driving postprandial AGE formation, lipid oxidation products from deep-frying polyunsaturated oils, and negligible anti-inflammatory omega-3 content from dory/basa fish
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dish to occasional consumption (monthly or less); the very high GL promotes postprandial inflammation via advanced glycation end-product formation, deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory lipid oxidation products, and the omega-3-poor dory/basa fish provides no anti-inflammatory EPA+DHA benefit — grilled oily fish with salad is a strongly preferred alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish and Chips — Singapore Restaurant Style is a popular Western-inspired dish served at Singapore casual dining restaurants and hawker Western stalls. It consists of a battered deep-fried white fish fillet (typically dory, basa, or barramundi — not the cod or haddock used in the UK original) served alongside thick-cut deep-fried potato chips and tartar sauce. The fish fillet (~120–180g cooked) is coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried in canola or sunflower oil until golden and crispy, producing a crunchy exterior with a soft, flaky fish interior. The chips are thick-cut and deep-fried to a soft-crispy texture. A standard plate weighs approximately 450g and delivers approximately 760 kcal, 28g protein, 38g fat (saturated fat ~6g), 72g carbohydrate, and ~900mg sodium. The glycemic load per plate is approximately 49 — one of the highest GL values among Singapore Western restaurant dishes — driven by the combined starch load of the wheat flour batter and potato chips. Singapore's use of dory/basa/barramundi means the dish provides negligible EPA+DHA omega-3 fatty acids (~0.2g per serving), unlike UK fish and chips made with cod or haddock. This dish is not suitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes (very high GL), hypertension (high sodium), or cardiovascular disease (high fat, high sodium, high caloric density). Grilled fish without batter, served with salad instead of chips, is a dramatically lower-GL and lower-sodium alternative. Source: component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Singapore. Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: how unhealthy is fish and chips

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (battered fried fish, deep-fried potato) and HPB Singapore, not from a direct measurement of the Singapore restaurant dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database weighted composite, not a direct measurement. Singapore restaurants use dory/basa/barramundi which differ nutritionally from cod/haddock used in published UK fish and chips data. Portion sizes vary across establishments. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite dish (battered fried dory + chips) is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore database at the composite dish level as of 2024; individual component fatty acid data exists but reliable weighted composite values for the Singapore restaurant preparation are not available. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite deep-fried dish is not reported in the primary sources used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for canola/sunflower oil frying; industrial trans fats are absent in this preparation). GI of 68 is a weighted analogous estimate (see gi_source); the dish is classified medium GI (55–69) but carries a very high GL of ~49 per standard plate. Portion size note: input record contained three portion entries with two duplicates (both 450g labelled 'grabfood') and one verified entry at 560g; the default serving size of 450g is used as the conservative standard restaurant portion for the core calculation (120g fish + 150g chips + 30g tartar sauce + 150g coleslaw/sides). The 560g verified entry (180g fish + 200g fries + 80g tartar sauce + 100g coleslaw) represents a larger restaurant serving and is noted for reference. Duplicate portion entries from input have been deduplicated. Singapore adaptation note: dory (Pangasius hypophthalmus) and basa are farmed freshwater fish with significantly lower omega-3 EPA+DHA content than marine cod or haddock used in traditional UK fish and chips.

improvement_actions_taken: Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi in local_names section.
Added Tamil and Hindi aliases to aliases array and aliases_by_language to satisfy G6 and Q8.
Corrected health_context condition key from 'diabetes' to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per scoring model requirement.
Added 'cardiovascular_disease' health context condition (was missing from input) with concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance.
Restructured gi_source from string to full object with title, organization, URL, accessed_date, source_match_type, and source_match_notes per RESULT template.
Restructured nutrient_source from inline source array to full object per RESULT template.
Mapped per_100g field names from input format (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) to template format (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, etc.).
Set monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g to -1 (data not available) and documented in notes per G3 negative sentinel rule.
Set water_g to -1 (data not available) and documented in notes.
Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 is justified (canola/sunflower oil, no industrial trans fats) and documented in notes.
Deduplicated portion entries (input had two identical 450g 'grabfood' entries).
Verified aliases_by_language consistency: all values appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
Expanded natural_language_description to exceed 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Added foodex2_gap_note and snomed_ct_gap_note fields to ontology section.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' (GI 68 is technically at the upper boundary of medium 55-69) — retained as medium per University of Sydney classification where medium = 55-69 inclusive.
